---
title: Observable JS
---

[Observable JS](https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/) can be used in Quarto to create
reactive browser-side figures and controls. This is useful when the data have already
been prepared, and learners should explore how different choices affect the displayed result.

The example below uses a small prepared table from the
[`airway`](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/data/experiment/html/airway.html)
RNA-seq experiment. The original experiment measured gene expression in human airway
smooth muscle cells with and without dexamethasone treatment
([Himes et al. 2014](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0099625), GEO `GSE52778`).

Here, the browser does not re-run DESeq2. Instead, Observable JS reacts to the controls
and filters a prepared differential expression result table.

## Differential expression explorer

Dexamethasone is a synthetic glucocorticoid. In airway cells, glucocorticoids
can change the transcription of genes involved in inflammation, cell adhesion,
stress responses, and airway remodeling. RNA-seq gives a readout of these changes
by counting reads assigned to each gene in treated and untreated samples.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: Mermaid diagram of the analysis pipeline. See [diagrams](diagrams.md) for more information on diagrams in Quarto. 
flowchart LR
  samples["Airway cells<br/>untreated vs<br/> dexamethasone-treated"]
  rnaseq["RNA-seq<br/>counts per gene"]
  table["Prepared results table"]
  volcano["Interactive volcano plot"]

  samples --> rnaseq
  rnaseq --> table
  table --> volcano
```

Differential expression analysis summarizes each gene with two quantities that
are useful to explore together:

- `log2FoldChange` describes the size and direction of the expression change.
  Positive values mean higher expression after dexamethasone treatment.
- `padj` is the adjusted p-value. Smaller values provide stronger evidence that
  the observed difference is not just sampling noise.

The volcano plot combines these two summaries. Genes far to the left or right
have larger expression changes, while genes higher on the plot have stronger
statistical evidence. The y-axis uses a compressed scale so that very small
adjusted p-values do not visually dominate the whole plot.

Move the thresholds to change which genes are called differentially expressed.
Search for a gene symbol or Ensembl ID to narrow the plot and table.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
airway = FileAttachment("data/airway_dex_de_results.csv").csv({typed: true})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof fdrThreshold = Inputs.range([0.001, 0.2], {
  value: 0.05,
  step: 0.001,
  label: "FDR threshold"
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof lfcThreshold = Inputs.range([0, 4], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "Minimum absolute log2 fold change"
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof geneQuery = Inputs.text({
  label: "Gene search",
  placeholder: "DUSP1, VCAM1, ENSG..."
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
annotated = airway.map((d) => {
  const significant = d.padj <= fdrThreshold && Math.abs(d.log2FoldChange) >= lfcThreshold;
  return {
    ...d,
    status: significant
      ? (d.log2FoldChange > 0 ? "Up" : "Down")
      : "Unchanged",
    negLog10Padj: -Math.log10(d.padj),
    plotNegLog10Padj: Math.log10(1 - Math.log10(d.padj))
  };
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
query = geneQuery.trim().toLowerCase()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
plotData = query
  ? annotated.filter((d) =>
      `${d.symbol} ${d.gene_id}`.toLowerCase().includes(query)
    )
  : annotated
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
summary = [
  {
    category: "Upregulated",
    genes: annotated.filter((d) => d.status === "Up").length
  },
  {
    category: "Downregulated",
    genes: annotated.filter((d) => d.status === "Down").length
  },
  {
    category: "Unchanged in this view",
    genes: annotated.filter((d) => d.status === "Unchanged").length
  }
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(summary)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  height: 430,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: {
    label: "log2 fold change, dexamethasone / untreated"
  },
  y: {
    label: "compressed -log10 adjusted p-value"
  },
  color: {
    domain: ["Up", "Down", "Unchanged"],
    range: ["#b94141", "#3c6ea8", "#9b9b9b"],
    legend: true
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#666"}),
    Plot.ruleX([-lfcThreshold, lfcThreshold], {
      stroke: "#666",
      strokeDasharray: "4,4"
    }),
    Plot.ruleY([Math.log10(1 - Math.log10(fdrThreshold))], {
      stroke: "#666",
      strokeDasharray: "4,4"
    }),
    Plot.dot(plotData, {
      x: "log2FoldChange",
      y: "plotNegLog10Padj",
      fill: "status",
      r: (d) => d.status === "Unchanged" ? 2.2 : 3.6,
      fillOpacity: 0.8,
      tip: true,
      title: (d) =>
        `${d.symbol || d.gene_id}\n${d.gene_id}\nlog2FC: ${d.log2FoldChange.toFixed(2)}\npadj: ${d.padj.toExponential(2)}`
    })
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filteredGenes = plotData
  .filter((d) => d.status !== "Unchanged")
  .sort((a, b) => a.padj - b.padj)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
tableRows = filteredGenes.map((d) => ({
  symbol: d.symbol || "",
  gene_id: d.gene_id,
  status: d.status,
  baseMean: Math.round(d.baseMean),
  log2FoldChange: d.log2FoldChange.toFixed(2),
  padj: d.padj.toExponential(2)
}))
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(tableRows)
```

Changing the FDR threshold changes how much statistical evidence is required.
Changing the fold-change threshold changes how large the expression difference
must be before it is highlighted. Both choices affect the final gene list, which
is why interactive thresholding is useful for teaching and exploratory analysis.
The plot caps very small adjusted p-values visually, but the thresholding and
The plot compresses very small adjusted p-values visually, but the thresholding
and table still use the original `padj` values.

## When to use OJS

OJS is a good fit when a Quarto page should provide fast, linked interactivity
over a prepared dataset. The sliders, plot, summary table, and filtered gene table
above all update in the browser after the page has been rendered.

Use Shiny, Pyodide, or another live execution approach instead when the interaction
needs to recompute an analysis from raw inputs, run Python or R code, or keep state
between larger workflows.

## Technical details

OJS cells use the chunk name `{ojs}`. A minimal control can be written like this:

````markdown
```{ojs}
viewof cutoff = Inputs.range([0, 1], {
  value: 0.05,
  step: 0.01,
  label: "Cutoff"
})
```
````

Other OJS cells can then use `cutoff` directly. When the slider changes, cells
that depend on `cutoff` update automatically in the browser.

For a prepared data file stored next to the page, load it with `FileAttachment`:

````markdown
```{ojs}
genes = FileAttachment("data/airway_dex_de_results.csv").csv({typed: true})
```
````

The `{typed: true}` option asks OJS to parse numeric columns as numbers instead
of strings.

The interactive example above hides the implementation cells with `echo: false`
so that only the controls and outputs are shown in the rendered page:

````markdown
```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filtered = genes.filter((d) => d.padj <= cutoff)
```
````

For a simple plot, Quarto makes Observable Plot available as `Plot`:

````markdown
```{ojs}
Plot.plot({
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(genes, {
      x: "log2FoldChange",
      y: (d) => -Math.log10(d.padj)
    })
  ]
})
```
````

No extra Quarto extension is needed for ordinary OJS cells in an HTML document.
The data file does need to be available in the rendered site, so keep it in a
path Quarto copies alongside the page.
